Paper summary:

```markdown
# Validity Extraction: LTZGLUE: The First Natural Language Understanding Benchmark for Luxembourgish
<!-- Model routing: Haiku (per-page extraction) → script (registry assembly) → Sonnet (narrative) -->

## Metadata
- **Title**: LTZGLUE: The First Natural Language Understanding Benchmark for Luxembourgish
- **Authors**: Alistair Plum, Felicia Körner, Anne-Marie Lutgen, Laura Bernardy, Fred Philippy, Emilia Milano, Nils Rehlinger, Cédric Lothritz, Tharindu Ranasinghe, Barbara Plank, Christoph Purschke
- **Venue/Year**: Not explicitly stated in registry (inferred ~2025–2026 based on citations)
- **Total Pages**: 17
- **Quotes Extracted**: 159

## Narrative Context

Interpretive prose organized by extraction category. Each factual claim references quote IDs from the registry. **This section is non-authoritative — it provides readability but is not evidence. Only the Quote Registry contains verbatim text from the paper.**

### 1. Task Taxonomy / Test Case Categories

LTZGLUE is presented as the first unified GLUE-style NLU benchmark for Luxembourgish, comprising eight tasks [Q1, Q8] that span binary and multi-class classification settings [Q2, Q11]. The eight tasks include headline acceptability (HA), sentiment analysis (SA), linguistic acceptability in binary and multi-class forms (LA-BINARY, LA-MULTI), named entity recognition (NER), news topic classification (TC), intent detection (ID), and recognizing textual entailment (RTE) [Q15, Q23, Q29, Q59, Q67]. The benchmark is explicitly modelled on the English GLUE benchmark [Q68], and the authors note that a substantial proportion of tasks are newly created for Luxembourgish rather than direct translations [Q69], with the suite designed to assess both fundamentally different modelling paradigms and task-specific phenomena [Q71].

From a deployment-validity standpoint, the topic taxonomy is a critical mismatch for the public-administration use case. The topic classification task covers only five editorial categories — SPORTS, CULTURE, TECHNOLOGY, BUSINESS, and ANIMALS [Q48, Q153] — drawn from RTL news [Q46], with no administrative, civic, housing, cross-border worker, or EU-institutional categories represented. The intent detection task covers a narrow set of reminder and alarm intents [Q154], derived from a voice-assistant corpus [Q51], not citizen-government communication. The RTE task is the most challenging overall [Q103], while topic classification is the easiest [Q99], suggesting that the benchmark's structural difficulty distribution may not align with the difficulty of administratively relevant language understanding tasks. The benchmark does cover NER [Q152] and sentiment analysis [Q149], which have partial relevance to the deployment, but only four entity types are retained after tag harmonisation (PER, ORG, LOC, MISC) [Q44], with no administrative jurisdiction or legal-status categories. Appendix prompts confirm the LLM zero-shot evaluation tasks [Q143, Q144, Q145, Q146, Q148, Q149, Q150, Q151, Q152, Q153, Q154, Q155], but none target administrative routing or civic-domain intent.

### 2. Data Sources and Collection

The benchmark draws on two primary text sources that underpin most tasks [Q12]: RTL (a Luxembourgish media outlet) providing news articles, radio transcripts, and user comments [Q74], and the Luxembourgish Online Dictionary (LOD) [Q30]. Additional sources include Wikipedia, web crawls from the Leipzig Collection, transcribed podcasts, transcribed political speeches from the Chambre des Députés, and Luxembourgish chat rooms [Q74, Q135]. The NER task merges two datasets: the automatically constructed JUDGEWEL corpus derived from Wikipedia and Wikidata [Q34, Q38], and a human-annotated corpus from RTL news comments by Lothritz et al. (2022) [Q40, Q45]. The sentiment analysis dataset draws on RTL commentary and letters to the editor [Q24], and the intent detection task is a human-translated adaptation of the English xSID dataset [Q51], with a machine-translated German training set [Q54]. The RTE task originates from a machine-translated Luxembourgish version of a standard entailment dataset, further refined using ChatGPT [Q60, Q66]. The authors acknowledge that LTZ linguistic data often originate from a limited set of public domains [Q119], a structural constraint of the low-resource setting. For the deployment context, this source profile is significant: RTL news and formal institutional text dominate, while citizen-correspondence registers, informal writing, and code-switched French-German-Luxembourgish mixtures are absent from nearly all task datasets.

### 3. Data Format and Preprocessing

Most benchmark tasks use plain text inputs at the sentence or document level, with the NER and intent detection tasks using BIO-tagged token sequences [Q35, Q57]. Preprocessing for headline acceptability involves TF-IDF-based nearest-neighbour negative headline swapping with temporal and lexical distance constraints [Q18, Q19, Q20, Q21, Q22], producing a train/dev/test split of 20k/3k/6k [Q21]. Linguistic acceptability uses a 70-10-20 split [Q32], and topic classification applies downsampling to mitigate class imbalance in the SPORTS category [Q49, Q50]. The RTE dataset was processed through ChatGPT-5.1 for quality improvement and then filtered, removing 22–28% of instances [Q61, Q66]. Non-Luxembourgish articles were removed from the topic classification dataset using the OpenLID language identifier [Q47], and sentences with fewer than three words were filtered from the pre-training corpus [Q75]. The intent detection BIO tags were transferred via fuzzy token matching followed by manual verification [Q57], and datasets are derived from publicly accessible sources with preprocessing that avoids directly identifying personal information [Q116]. For the encoder models, both LTZ-E1-mini and LTZ-E1-base use a BPE tokenizer trained on the full pre-training set with a vocabulary of 50,368 tokens and a maximum sequence length of 1,024 [Q128, Q129]. The text inputs throughout are formal, edited prose from news and institutional sources, with no documented preprocessing to handle orthographic variation or code-switching — a meaningful gap for the deployment's expected input distribution of colloquial, non-standardized citizen messages [Q7, Q112].

### 4. Label Categories and Output Types

The label ontology across LTZGLUE tasks is entirely single-label and hard-classification. Binary tasks include headline acceptability (True/False) [Q148, Q147], linguistic acceptability binary (0/1) [Q33, Q150], and textual entailment (0/1) [Q155]. Multi-class tasks include sentiment analysis (positive, neutral, negative) [Q149], linguistic acceptability multi-class (correct, verb, adj, syntax, ortho) [Q31, Q151], topic classification (sports, culture, technology, business, animals) [Q48, Q153], intent detection with a fixed set of reminder/alarm intents [Q154], and NER with BIO tags covering LOC, PER, DATE, ORG, and MISC [Q152]. The NER tag set was harmonised by merging GPE and LOC into a single location label [Q44], reducing administrative granularity. For the deployment context, the output ontology presents three critical gaps: (1) there are no administrative, housing, cross-border worker, or civic categories in the topic taxonomy; (2) all tasks emit single hard labels, whereas the deployment requires multi-label outputs with calibrated confidence scores [Q4 elicitation]; and (3) the sentiment labels (positive/neutral/negative) are generic and not calibrated to the formal-but-understated register of citizen-government correspondence, as discussed further under annotation. The binary label encoding for true/false is documented at the prompt level [Q147], but no probabilistic or ranked output format is supported anywhere in the benchmark.

### 5. Annotation Process

Annotation practices vary substantially across tasks and raise important validity concerns for the deployment context. The sentiment analysis task was annotated by two native speakers of Luxembourgish [Q25], instructed to use an "unsure" category only when they would otherwise assign labels randomly [Q26], with Cohen's Kappa of 0.45 [Q27] — a moderate agreement score that falls below standard thresholds for reliable annotation, and disagreements were resolved by annotator consensus [Q28]. The intent detection dataset was translated by a single native LTZ speaker with consultation of additional speakers in ambiguous cases [Q52]. The RTE dataset underwent a two-step LLM-based quality and label verification process using ChatGPT-5-Mini [Q63, Q64], with manual correction of cases where automatic quality improvement inadvertently altered logical contradictions [Q65]. The JUDGEWEL NER dataset relied on LLMs as judges with minimal human verification [Q37], while the Lothritz et al. NER dataset was fully human-annotated [Q42]. The paper closes with a brief acknowledgement of student assistant annotators [Q114], without detailing their linguistic background, residency, or familiarity with administrative registers.

From a deployment-validity perspective (HIGH priority for OC), the annotator provenance is incompletely documented. The sentiment analysis Cohen's Kappa of 0.45 [Q27] is particularly concerning: moderate agreement on a three-class task from only two annotators — with no documentation of their familiarity with Luxembourgish administrative communication norms — cannot reliably validate sentiment labels for formal citizen correspondence. The absence of annotators drawn from civil-service or government-adjacent backgrounds means the ground-truth labels may not align with how civil servants would interpret tone and urgency. The use of LLMs as proxies for human judgment in quality filtering and label verification [Q63, Q64] introduces additional uncertainty about whether labels reflect genuine Luxembourgish linguistic norms or model-induced artefacts.

### 6. Evaluation Metrics and Output Modality

All tasks in LTZGLUE are evaluated using F1 scores [Q93, Q96], with macro-F1 reported for prompted LLMs evaluated once [Q96] and averaged F1 over three runs with standard deviations reported for encoder-based models [Q89, Q96]. Class-balanced loss is applied during fine-tuning for imbalanced tasks (linguistic acceptability and sentiment analysis) [Q92]. Invalid or malformed LLM outputs are discarded prior to evaluation [Q90, Q91]. Hyperparameter selection is performed via Bayesian search with early stopping, capped at 30 runs per model-task combination [Q138], with best hyperparameters selected on the validation set [Q137]. Full results for all eight tasks across validation and test sets are reported in appendix tables [Q156, Q158, Q159].

Key findings are that MMBERT-base achieves the highest and most stable overall performance [Q97, Q105], prompted LLMs substantially underperform fine-tuned encoders in aggregate [Q98], topic classification is the easiest task with near-encoder-level LLM performance [Q99, Q100], intent detection strongly favours language-specific models [Q101, Q102], and RTE is the hardest task with LLMs outperforming most encoders [Q104]. For the deployment, the exclusive reliance on single-label hard F1 scoring [Q84, Q93] means the benchmark provides no calibration data for confidence-scored or multi-label outputs, which are operationally required for the routing logic. The zero-shot LLM evaluation described [Q83, Q85] is methodologically relevant to the deployment scenario, but results should be treated as indicative rather than directly comparable to supervised settings [Q86]. Compute details are documented [Q133, Q140] but are not directly validity-relevant.

### 7. Stated Limitations

The authors are unusually candid about the limitations of LTZGLUE, and many of these limitations map directly onto the deployment's validity concerns. Luxembourgish is characterised as an under-researched language with approximately 400,000 speakers [Q4], classified as "scraping-by" in Joshi et al.'s typology [Q13], with only a handful of existing NLU tasks [Q5] and most of those concentrated in the news domain with fewer than a thousand instances each [Q6]. Ongoing standardisation of the language and vast sociolinguistic variation further complicate reliable evaluation [Q7].

For the deployment's HIGH-priority IC and IO dimensions, the authors explicitly acknowledge that "coverage across domains, registers, and demographic varieties may also be limited" and that "most data sources reflect formal writing or institutional usage and therefore do not fully represent informal and multilingual contexts" [Q112]. This is a direct admission that the benchmark's input distribution does not cover the code-switched, orthographically variable language that characterises citizen messages. The intent detection task's source register — user commands for a voice-controlled AI assistant — is acknowledged to have no equivalent reference corpus in Luxembourgish [Q55], and terminology could not be systematically verified [Q56].

The authors also flag that some benchmark data may have been included in the pre-training corpora of evaluated LLMs, potentially inflating performance estimates [Q111], and that prompt-based evaluation introduces variability from prompt sensitivity and instruction-following differences [Q86], warranting interpretation as indicative only [Q87]. Benchmark performance should not be used as evidence of cultural or demographic coverage [Q121], models may reproduce dominant norms while under-representing regional, sociolectal, or multilingual practices [Q120], and societal biases present in institutional media data may influence model behaviour in deployment [Q117, Q118]. The authors caution specifically against deploying systems trained on LTZGLUE in public-facing settings without inspecting task-specific subsets [Q123]. Additional limitations include annotation-induced label imbalance and potential biases from automatic preprocessing [Q113], incomplete removal of harmful content by automated filtering [Q122], and the acknowledgement that the pre-training set is small [Q132] with hyperparameter ranges kept simple for computational feasibility [Q139].

### 8. Authors and Affiliations

The research team spans four institutions across three countries [Q10]: the University of Luxembourg (Luxembourg), LMU Munich and the Munich Center for Machine Learning (Germany), LIST (Luxembourg Institute of Science and Technology), and Lancaster University (UK). The project is supported by the LLMs4EU grant under the EU Digital Europe Programme, and two co-authors (Körner and Plank) are additionally supported by the ERC Consolidator Grant DIALECT [Q115]. Two new encoder models — LTZ-E1-mini (68M parameters) and LTZ-E1-base (110M parameters) — are contributed alongside the benchmark [Q72, Q73], following the Ettin/ModernBERT recipe. Baseline models evaluated include mBERT [Q77], a more recent multilingual BERT variant (MMBERT) [Q78], LUXEMBERT [Q79], XLM-RoBERTa [Q80], and a set of prompted LLMs including QWEN3-235B, LLAMA-3.3, GEMMA-3-27B, and GPT5-Mini [Q81]. The institutional footprint — primarily academic, Luxembourg- and Germany-based — is consistent with a research team well-positioned to understand Luxembourgish linguistic norms, though the absence of explicit government-domain or civil-service domain expertise among listed affiliations is relevant: none of the institutions are public-administration bodies, suggesting that the benchmark's construction reflects academic NLP priorities (GLUE-style task diversity) rather than administrative routing priorities (civic topic coverage, register diversity, multi-label output).
```

| Q80 | 6 | authors_affiliations | "Finally, we evaluate XLM-RoBERTa (XLM-R-base) (Conneau et al., 2020), a large-scale multilingual model trained on substantially more data and languages than MBERT-base, and commonly used as a strong reference point for multilingual NLU." |
| Q81 | 6 | authors_affiliations | "In addition to supervised encoder-based models, we evaluate a set of LLMs in a prompt-based zero-shot setting. | Q82 | 6 | stated_limitations | "None of these models are fine-tuned on LTZGLUE, although some of the text data (RTL, Wikipedia) is very likely to have been processed during training." |

| Q84 | 6 | evaluation_metrics | "We did not use a Multiple Choice Question Answering (MCQA)-setup, but provided the labels that should be used as output." |
| Q85 | 6 | evaluation_metrics | "This evaluation setting reflects the growing use of LLMs as general-purpose language understanding systems, particularly in scenarios where annotated data is scarce or unavailable." |
| Q86 | 6 | stated_limitations | "However, prompt-based evaluation introduces additional sources of variability, including prompt sensitivity and differences in instruction-following behaviour across models. | Q105 | 8 | evaluation_metrics | "First, MMBERT-base consistently achieves the strongest or near-strongest performance across almost all tasks, combining high mean F1 scores with comparatively low variance, suggesting that broad multilingual pre-training with sufficient LTZ exposure yields stable and transferable representations." |

| Q108 | 9 | task_taxonomy | "This paper makes two central contributions to LTZ NLU. First, we introduce a new benchmark that provides the first comprehensive GLUE-style evaluation suite for LTZ. Second, we present a systematic evaluation of encoder-based models and prompted large language models across all tasks, offering concrete guidance on model choice in such a low-resource setting." |
| Q109 | 9 | data_sources | "The construction of the dataset required a deliberately resource-conscious approach. In the absence of large, task-diverse annotated resources, we combine the reuse of existing datasets with the targeted annotation of new data, carefully aligning annotation schemes across tasks, and using large language models as auxiliary tools." |
| Q110 | 9 | stated_limitations | "While LTZGLUE provides the first systematic benchmark for LTZ NLU, the dataset remains constrained by the availability and scope of existing resources. Several tasks rely on relatively small or domain-specific corpora, which limits the ecological validity of the results and restricts the range of linguistic phenomena covered." |
| Q111 | 9 | stated_limitations | "In addition, some of the data sources used in this benchmark may already be included, in whole or in part, in the pre-training corpora of the large language models evaluated in this work. While the exact composition of proprietary pre-training datasets is typically not fully disclosed, this potential overlap cannot be entirely ruled out and may inflate performance estimates." |
| Q112 | 9 | stated_limitations | "Coverage across domains, registers, and demographic varieties may also be limited. LTZ displays substantial orthographic and sociolinguistic variation, yet most data sources reflect formal writing or institutional usage and therefore do not fully represent informal and multilingual contexts." |
| Q113 | 9 | stated_limitations | "Although we draw on established GLUE-style tasks, some annotation decisions and class distributions are necessarily influenced by resource constraints. | Q117 | 10 | stated_limitations | "However, some tasks draw on data originally produced in institutional or media contexts, which may reflect societal biases in representation." |
| Q118 | 10 | stated_limitations | "These patterns can influence model behaviour and should be considered when deploying systems trained on LTZGLUE." |
| Q119 | 10 | data_sources | "LTZ is a small language community, and linguistic data often originate from a limited set of public domains." |
| Q120 | 10 | stated_limitations | "As a result, models may reproduce dominant norms while under-representing regional, sociolectal, or multilingual practices." |

| Q123 | 10 | stated_limitations | "Researchers using LTZGLUE are encouraged to inspect task-specific subsets and consider downstream implications, especially in public-facing settings." |
| Q124 | 12 | task_taxonomy | "For demonstration purposes, we present an example for each task in ltzGLUE in Table 7. The examples are intended to illustrate the task formulations and typical model inputs and outputs." |
| Q125 | 12 | data_format | "We follow the Ettin recipe (Weller et al., 2026), based on ModernBERT (Warner et al., 2025), for training hyperpameters and model architecture." |
| Q126 | 12 | data_format | "We train two sizes of LTZ-E1 models, mini and base, with 68M and 110M non-embedding parameters, respectively." |
| Q127 | 12 | data_format | "LTZ-E1-mini has 19 hidden layers, a hidden size of 512, an intermediate size of 768, and 8 attention heads, whereas LTZ-E1-base has 22 hidden layers, a hidden size of 768, an intermediate size of 1152, and 12 attention heads." |
| Q128 | 12 | data_format | "Both models share a GPTNeoXTokenizerFast tokenizer (Black et al., 2022), a BPE-based tokenizer, which we train on the entire pre-training set, using a minimum frequency of two and a vocabulary size of 50,368." |
| Q129 | 12 | data_format | "We use a constant batch size of 1024 packed sequences, where both models have a max sequence length of 1024." |
| Q130 | 12 | data_format | "We follow ModernBERT (Warner et al., 2025) and Ettin (Weller et al., 2026) in using the Warmup-Stable-Decay (WSD) scheduler (Zhai et al., 2022; Hu et al., 2024), though we use a shorter warmup and decay phase of 500 batches each, due to our smaller pre-training dataset size and larger number of epochs (10 vs. one)." |
| Q131 | 12 | data_format | "Again following ModernBERT and Ettin's recipe, we use the StableAdamW optimizer (Wortsman et al., 2023), with a peak learning rate of 3e-3 with a weight decay of 3e-4 for LTZ-E1-mini and 8e-4 with a weight decay of 1e-5 for LTZ-E1-base." |
| Q132 | 12 | stated_limitations | "As our pre-training set is small, we" |
| Q133 | 13 | evaluation_metrics | "We use a 20GB MIG partition of an NVIDIA A100-SXM4-80GB to pretrain each model, taking 47 hours for LTZ-E1-mini and 76 hours for LTZ-E1-base." |
| Q134 | 13 | stated_limitations | "However, we note that compute times were negatively impacted by concurrent jobs on the server cluster with suboptimal CPU thread management." |

| Q136 | 13 | evaluation_metrics | "Though we do not aim to optimise performance in our evaluation, we conduct basic hyperparameter sweeps for each model and task combination in order to provide a fairer comparison across models." |
| Q137 | 13 | evaluation_metrics | "For each model and task combination, we select the best hyperparameters based on the validation set, and use those parameters to finetune two additional models with differing seeds, resulting in three runs." |
| Q138 | 13 | evaluation_metrics | "In order to reduce the computational demand of the sweeps, we use Bayesian search with early stopping after three iterations, and cap each sweep at 30 runs, for 1,440 total runs across all models and tasks (and an additional 96 to finetune the two additional seeds)." |
| Q139 | 13 | stated_limitations | "However, we note again that these ranges were kept simple to keep sweeps computationally feasible, thus, these values should not be seen as optimal hyperparameters." |
